words: When he left town. I don't think he went... He never came up on the hill or something. They went to him, you know. He couldn't... People liked him. Oh, sure. Old Gene Chinaman. You bet.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Wasn't celery a specialty of his?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Yeah. Yep, celery. The British town, celery. Quite a gene.

speaker: Martha Long
words: Celery was a luxury then, wasn't it?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Yeah. Oh, sure. You had to shove it in your...

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Did you yet? What happened to Gene?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Well, them Chinaman's, you know, have to go back to China and die. They had got a few Chinaman's in Lewiston, and so they finally... He come up and got him. Frickin' probably very different stories than the truth of them. He was wearing Chinaman's, and I knew I asked about him. I think he was right about it, but he died going across. So you have to be buried in China.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Was he fairly old before he left?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Oh, yeah, yeah. Yeah, he was old. Gene Chinaman.

speaker: Martha Long
words: Didn't he go by?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Oh, yeah, probably by.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Was he dead when you came here?

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: No, I didn't know him. I mean, he wasn't here any more. He come up there one day, oh, he was mad. So I'm just...

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Damn, will you show him the story there?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: He... I was working for him. Damn, well... He went and heard a little spud some spring. What kind of... Early rows. Potatoes. It was just a plant. So damn, he was giving... He was early potatoes all right, but they went and heard a rose. He come back up the store with them potatoes. He had them just in his hand. Oh, he was mad. Him, no early rows. Him, no early rows. But he was mad.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: What kind do you think they were?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Oh, it was an early potato. I don't know what the hell, but there was an early rose. They were early rows. No, he didn't, but he wanted early rows. He knew what he wanted. Because you were Chinaman, you couldn't fool him. Him, no early rows. He didn't call him a him. Oh, yes. Because we used to... His kids used to go down and visit him.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Nobody ever teased him or anything.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: He was too nice to everybody. And everybody. It depended on him first. Vegetables, you know. Anything fresh.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Yes, you don't see them anymore. They don't come...

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Do they come out to this country anymore?

speaker: Lee Magnuson0:41:33
timestamp: Not too many, I don't think.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Probably.

speaker: Martha Long
words: I got the quote of them, huh?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: I don't know. Produce business has changed for one thing, so they don't come to the little towns that used to.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Are they in the bigger places?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Well, in the Chinatown, you know. Lots of Chinese people that... You know, even these little towns, you know, we had a lot of time we'd have laundry here, too. Chinese laundry. Oh, yeah. And they'd come up, you know, and come to Lewis and then come up to Jean's and they'd run a laundry here. But they never seemed to get that start and made that pay like Jean did in his garden. People wouldn't... Of course, there wasn't enough people had laundry work done.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Were there many Indians around Kendrick? Did they just come through or...?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: They just come through the shop. They'd come up here in the tree and come through. When we seen the Indians most of the time was when they... In the summertime, when they was going back into the woods for half the huckleberry season. And then on we'd have quite a... Coming through, going up, had four cars on horse and buggy days. And after the cars, we had the end of the Indian business.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Go to Lewiston and I guess...

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: There ain't any Indians anymore, is there?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Not very many.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: You're all Americans, ain't there?

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Well, sure.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Well, that's what I mean, there. Live like the Americans are no thin. This young generation... Do the things I guess they do. Yeah. We don't see them anymore. They used to come through. They'd come up here and trade because... Because it was easier to come up with teams, you know. The Kendrick would be to go to Lewis and... With their teams. I think that was the reason of it. Of course they got cars. We never seen Indians anymore for this trade territory. Only when they'd gone through the huckleberries. But there used to be quite a few Indian families between Juliaetta and there. There's a lot of Indians down there. They'd come up here and trade.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: That's right, huh?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: No, that's a string. Like mine.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Do you like them?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: I guess they...

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Hold better than ... don't they?

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Yeah, I think so.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: What were prices when you first went into dry goods? What would shoes and clothes cost? When I first went in? What would a pair of boots cost?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Well... You'd buy a pretty good pair of shoes. The one they got up past $5, it was using too much money. We used to sell quite a few, push it with the other stores. The dress shoes were super ones, just three, three and a half.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: But good shoes never got much below $5. Of course, like floor signs and things like that, they'd better make the shoes at a lower price.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: What is it now, $25?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: At least.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: How about shirts and things like that?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Well, I don't think it's too much difference there. I don't know what a good shirt costs now. Well, they're up $8 and $10 now or more. Well, I imagine shirts and nuggets is about a good shirt. We used to put out a pretty good shirt for $1 .95. You know, but it wasn't as good as shirts made nowadays.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: People didn't have the money, they wasn't after the dry price merchandise.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Dry side and the money. I'd hate to be here running the store now. I don't see how Brayden makes it over there, that stock.

speaker: Martha Long
words: Didn't you sell lots of suits that you took orders for in those days?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Quite a few, yeah. Yeah. Well, we had clothing when I had Baker's store. I used to work for them for a tenor store company. We carried about 300 suits.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Well, I wanted to ask you about, tell about the time President Taft came to town.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Didn't I ever tell you that story?

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: You told me, but tell it for the...

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Tape?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Tape. Tape. Well, them days the trains always carried the passenger trains.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: The last car was the, what do you call them?

speaker: Martha Long
words: Observation.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: What?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Observation. Observation cars. And they had a platform in the back, you know, where you could go out and look. The country coming through, or you could get a train stopped right there. So he, when he comes through, he comes out of the car onto this weekend. We're in the train, start giving his speech, and he's about half asleep, he's sounding like it. He says,

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: when I come up through this beautiful valley of yours and... He's touching it. And all the fruit trees and everything, I don't think he ever looked out, because he wouldn't meet many fruit trees during here in the most. And this time there's one big orchard to ride out of us. And... And he says it. He just made me feel like a big red apple.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Here's a big pot of gunpowder.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: You've seen a picture of that, you know?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: That's why I just feel like a big red apple.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Speaking of presidents, what I remember most about seeing a president with Theodore Roosevelt come to Moscow, and of course, he let out, and let everybody go that could go up to share him. And... And we heard him speak on top of that. He had a great big... For as reason as his father, he brought a great big stack of wheat and sacks, you know, sack wheat. And I can remember that in his pal, and that is the platform he's speaking. He spoke from this platform, that Roosevelt did.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: That's what I remember about him. Yeah, you know.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Did you ride the train up? Is that how you got up there?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Yeah, oh sure.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: We went to Moscow more than we did to... And he... And then, listen, because we could go up to Moscow in the morning on the 9 o 'clock, and come back at the night train, you know, and you could have a whole day up there. You'd be back down there about 9 o 'clock, the train just coming, like 9, 10 o 'clock. But coming to Lewiston, you know, up here, they'd come up on 9 o 'clock. Well, there's a lot of... quite a few people coming, the Indians come train, but unless they waited for the night train, they'd have to go back over here about 12 o 'clock to catch the train going back. There's one come up at 4 o 'clock in the evening.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Wouldn't cost very much, would it, to ride to Moscow?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Well, I think... I think it's a little less than a dollar, if I remember right. It's a round dollar, pretty close to a dollar.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: So you still didn't do it real often?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Oh, I'm turning right you didn't do it real often. You didn't have the money to do a lot of things real often. You know, but them days it's different. Now, if you wait till you need a pair of shoes, you're going to buy a pair of shoes. And if you wait till you need a shirt, you're going to buy a shirt. But in them days, you went and bought your two seasons, you bought a clothes. You fixed up for the fall and you fixed up for the summer, spring. And that's... that was the end of our shopping. It wasn't any more shopping than buying.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: We had... most people, kids I run with and everything got all their stuff from mailer house, but gosh, we never had any luck buying from mailer house. If we bought anything, we'd have sent it back, wouldn't fit or something. Gosh.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: I know one time mother, of course, did her own stuff for us in the fall. She only tried it once.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: I don't think they do the mailer business they used to do.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Oh, it's pretty big.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Is it?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Yeah.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Didn't tell them for talks. Yeah. Well, no, but they didn't like it. It was no mistake.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: You can't get as many different things. You can't get... They sold, you know, they sold up to wagons down tools and everything, you know, they don't...

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: They don't anymore, huh?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Not as much as that, I don't know.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Well, they sold that, and they ate too. Yeah.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: What was the story about the blacksmith when you were a little boy?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: A fellow named Hodges used witter and the wood dark place.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: And I used to stick around, we were pretty good pals. I was just a kid.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: And give me a job, I'd make it. Them days there was a lot of wagon and hack work, you know, tightening up wheels. They made wedges, I don't know what they called them. Well, it was a wheel, wedges for spokes, wedges for spokes, you know, after they put spokes in, you know, on the rim. They put them wedges in there, tighten it before they put the...

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: What do you call them?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Tire. Tire. Steel tire. You know, you used to go over there and make these hardwood wedges. Oh. The hands, you know, it's so... Some blocks, and I'd make it with that. I'd draw a knife, I'd make these wedges. Hardwood wedges. He'd pay me with a hundred.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: How many would you make in a day?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Oh, I don't know. I don't remember that. I made quite a few. I made them until I got tired. Oh, I made a hundred maybe. Maybe two hundred, I don't remember.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: You were still in Leland then, right?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Yeah, weirdly. Well, I was an old kind of business.

speaker: Martha Long
words: What about the clothes that man made that black slip?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Oh, yeah. He was always made... He was a clown. And he'd black himself up, you know. He was really a clown. He was a good one, too. But he made this doll, wooden doll, and jointed the legs and the arms. And he put this thing on a board after he made it, and he had a whole board in the back of it, you know, and a handle put on him. So he'd hold this.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: He'd hold it with one hand out on this board, and he'd pound it, make a batch with it. We'd hit it in the board, you know, hammer or something, maybe. And he'd make that thing, oh boy, he'd really make that thing dance. That was cute. It was really short. He was always clowning. He was a character.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: He liked the kids?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Oh, he loved them. He all loved him, too. He didn't have any kids of his own. Was he older then, or was he... Oh, yeah. He was grown, married. He married one... Finally, I don't know whether he was married before he come to Leland or not. He was a pastor then, but he married one of the Huffman girls up here on the hill. Yeah, he died. She died.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: She died, and he never got married again after that.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Gee, I think there isn't any names or any people left up there anymore. All them people used to be around Leland. There's not much there. One or two farmers owned it all.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: What all was in Leland when you lived there? What businesses?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: It would be easier to tell you what business wasn't there. There was just everything. Well, there was a big general store, and it was the biggest anything in Kendrick. He went in to Gosford, and one time there was two hardwares up there, but there was always a hardware store. They sold machinery, and then there was a drug store and a butcher shop. We had the butcher shop and a big general store. Sometimes there was another store, but the only one that you went in to Gosford was one that really mad the business and made it there. They made a pile of money there. And the floor mill. They made floor there. Van Pelt Mill. You've probably heard of that. Yeah.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: You told us about his phone.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: His telephone. Yeah. That's the reason he had this phone to keep the market on the wheat.

speaker: Martha Long
words: Was there a barber shop?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Yeah. One time.

speaker: Martha Long
words: Dr.

speaker: Martha Long
words: Dentist?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Two slums. One time. Churches. Three churches. One time.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: The UB Church never got started hardly. It never got going there. There's South Memphis and North Memphis. Democrats on Long Sides Street and the Republicans on the other. That's a fact.

speaker: Lee Magnuson
words: Which one were you going to? What did he think?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: I'll let you set your mind on it.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: My dad had turned over in his grave if he was going over to North Memphis Church. See the north and south. One was on South Sides Street and the other. One was on the north. One catered to niggas and the other didn't. Didn't have any niggas. That's right though. All the Republicans down there at the NE North Church. South Memphis, all Democrats.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: Well, I guess just the same way it is yet, ain't it?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: North and South is. Well, it's changing.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: What?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: I bet it isn't back. Not as straight. Not in the south, I bet it ain't changing. They're still Democrats. I'll say they're Democrats. They don't get a vote and they'll see where they're elected to you.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: What do you think of our president? Is he going to make it again?

words: I don't know. It's kind of shaky.

words: Who's going to run against him?

words: I don't know. I'm waiting for Senator Church. Well, I know, but he won't...

words: Church will be on Democratic ticket.

words: Well, he won't have no trouble in the primary, Mr.

words: Well, they're not even sure about that.

words: He was pretty close in New Hampshire.

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: When you were a kid.

words: When I first came to Kendrick, there was a post office at Bleelyn, one at Cameron, one at Southwick, one at... Did I say... one at Cabbany's and one at Tekin.

words: How many is that? One?

speaker: Martha Long
words: Five.

speaker: F. Marvin Long
words: At Tekin. And then there was... London and Crescent and Park. There was three more.

words: Is that eight?

speaker: F. Marvin Long or Martha Long
words: Excellent. Then there was all post offices.
